prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response:
“**Hallelujah**” by Leonard Cohen is the song that most profoundly moves me, particularly Jeff Buckley’s transcendent 1994 rendition.

It works on me because it feels like a map of the human soul. It holds biblical imagery, shattered romance, and spiritual longing all in the same fragile vessel. Cohen’s lyrics masterfully intertwine the sacred and the profane — the “holy dove” and the “broken halo” — acknowledging that transcendence and brokenness are not opposites, but often the same thing. Buckley’s performance embodies this perfectly: his voice is both a vulnerable whisper and a soaring cry, turning the song into a five-minute pilgrimage from doubt to a kind of weary, beautiful acceptance. It doesn’t offer easy answers; it offers the grace of asking the questions themselves. That’s why it feels eternally true.
